narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path despite the challenges many face, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict whether someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 54% chance that you would not—which crossed the threshold for denial, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 stands out positively, above the typical 3.21 average, and it was one of the strongest factors pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Similarly, your school deciles of 4th in Year 1 and Year 3—roughly average compared to others—also nudged things slightly in a favorable direction, suggesting solid performance relative to your peers. Your choice to attend full-time, like most applicants at 92.7%, and your middle family income bracket provided minor positive boosts too. However, the single biggest reason tipping the scales toward failure was your LSAT score of 25, well below the average of 36.70, which had the most powerful influence in pushing the model to predict a struggle on the bar exam. Overall, while your GPA and school rankings helped balance things out better than for many, that lower LSAT score carried the heaviest weight, outweighing those strengths in the model's eyes when compared to typical successful students who often have higher scores across the board. This doesn't reflect your full potential or resilience as a Black woman breaking barriers—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, as even a modest improvement could flip predictions for other schools, and pair it with highlighting your unique story in appeals or new applications. Gathering letters emphasizing your grit and any bar prep experience could also strengthen your case. You're capable of this—many in similar situations have succeeded by targeting their weakest area head-on. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours.
